overview.brief_description: Vigo is a vibrant coastal city in the northwest of Spain, known for its bustling port, rich maritime heritage, and proximity to stunning natural landscapes. With a blend of modern urban life and traditional Galician culture, Vigo offers visitors a dynamic experience by the Atlantic Ocean.
why_visit: Explore beautiful beaches and the nearby Cíes Islands, part of the Atlantic Islands of Galicia National Park
Experience fresh seafood and the renowned Galician cuisine in local markets and restaurants
Discover the lively old town with its historic architecture and vibrant plazas
Enjoy outdoor activities such as hiking, sailing, and birdwatching in scenic coastal settings
overview.best_time_to_visit: May to September, when the weather is warm and sunny, ideal for beach activities and outdoor exploration

seasonal_notes.spring: Mild temperatures and blossoming landscapes make it perfect for hiking and cultural events.
seasonal_notes.summer: Warm and sunny, with peak tourism and lively beach life; ideal for water sports and festivals.
seasonal_notes.autumn: Cooler weather with fewer tourists, great for exploring museums and enjoying local gastronomy.
seasonal_notes.winter: Mild and rainy, quieter with a more relaxed pace, suitable for indoor cultural experiences.

practical_info.visit_duration: 3 to 4 days to fully experience city highlights and nearby natural attractions
practical_info.getting_around: Public buses, taxis, and bike rentals are common; walking is enjoyable in the city center
practical_info.safety: Generally very safe for tourists, though standard precautions against petty theft are advised

must_do: Visit the Cíes Islands for pristine beaches and hiking trails
Stroll along the vibrant old town, including Praza da Constitución
Explore the Maritime Museum of Galicia to learn about local seafaring history
Sample fresh seafood at the O Berbés fish market
Walk along the scenic promenade of Samil Beach
worth_doing: Take a boat tour of the Ría de Vigo estuary
Visit Castrelos Park and the Quiñones de León Museum
Discover the modern architecture of the City of Justice building
Attend a local festival such as the Festas de San Roque
Explore the nearby town of Baiona and its medieval fortress
hidden_gems: Hike the coastal trail from Vigo to Redondela for stunning sea views
Visit the small fishing village of Alcabre for authentic local atmosphere
Explore the ruins of Santa Tegra Celtic settlement overlooking the city
Discover local art at the MARCO Contemporary Art Museum
Enjoy a quiet afternoon in the Rosalía de Castro Park

sites.name: Castro Fortress (Castelo do Castro)
sites.description: Perched atop a hill overlooking Vigo, Castro Fortress offers panoramic views of the city and the Ría de Vigo. Originally a Celtic settlement, it was fortified by the Romans and later expanded in the 17th century to defend against pirate attacks.
sites.seasonal_notes: Best visited in spring and autumn for mild weather and clear views
sites.booking_tips: No booking required; arrive early to avoid crowds

sites.name: Museo do Mar de Galicia
sites.description: This modern maritime museum explores Galicia’s deep connection with the sea through interactive exhibits, historic artifacts, and aquariums showcasing local marine life. It highlights Vigo’s fishing heritage and shipbuilding industry.
sites.seasonal_notes: Ideal for rainy or hot days
sites.booking_tips: Tickets can be purchased onsite; group tours available on request

sites.name: O Castro Park (Parque do Castro)
sites.description: This green space surrounding the Castro Fortress is perfect for leisurely strolls and picnics. It features ancient ruins, several lookout points, and well-maintained walking paths shaded by native trees.
sites.seasonal_notes: Spring and autumn offer lush foliage and pleasant temperatures

sites.name: Concatedral de Santa María de Vigo
sites.description: This neo-Gothic co-cathedral, dating from the early 20th century, stands as one of Vigo’s most architecturally impressive religious buildings. Its stained glass windows and ornate altarpieces attract both worshippers and visitors interested in religious art.
sites.seasonal_notes: Open year-round; major festivals occur in late summer

sites.name: Praza da Constitución
sites.description: The main square in Vigo’s old town, Praza da Constitución is surrounded by historic buildings and bustling cafes. It has been the social heart of the city since medieval times and hosts seasonal markets and cultural events.
sites.seasonal_notes: Vibrant during summer festivals and Christmas markets

sites.name: Mercado da Pedra
sites.description: Located near the port, Mercado da Pedra is famous for its fresh seafood stalls and vibrant atmosphere. It’s popular among locals for oysters, clams, and other shellfish, often consumed on-site at informal bars.
sites.seasonal_notes: Best in autumn and winter when seafood is at its peak
sites.booking_tips: No booking required; arrive early for freshest catches

visit_profile.weather_fallback: Indoor Mercado de la Piedra bars

sites.name: Parque de Castrelos
sites.description: The largest urban park in Vigo, Castrelos Park features beautiful gardens, a lake, and the Pazo Quiñones de León, a historic palace turned museum. It’s a favorite spot for locals to relax and for families to enjoy outdoor activities.
sites.seasonal_notes: Magnificent flower blooms in spring and summer

sites.name: Pazo Quiñones de León
sites.description: This 17th-century Baroque palace within Castrelos Park is home to an important collection of Galician art and artifacts. The palace gardens are a highlight, featuring sculptures and traditional Galician architecture.
sites.seasonal_notes: Ideal during spring and summer when gardens are in bloom
sites.booking_tips: Tickets available onsite; group tours by appointment

sites.name: Islas Cíes (Cíes Islands)
sites.description: Part of the Atlantic Islands of Galicia National Park, the Cíes Islands feature pristine beaches, crystal-clear waters, and rich biodiversity. Accessible by ferry from Vigo, the islands are perfect for hiking, bird watching, and relaxing on some of Spain’s best beaches.
sites.seasonal_notes: Open April to October; closed in winter
sites.booking_tips: Book ferry tickets in advance especially in summer; limited daily visitors

sites.name: Rúa de Príncipe
sites.description: The main pedestrian shopping thoroughfare in Vigo, Rúa de Príncipe is lined with boutiques, cafes, and historic buildings. It’s ideal for people-watching, tasting local tapas, and soaking in the lively urban atmosphere.
sites.seasonal_notes: Bustles year-round, especially during Christmas season

sites.name: Parque Central de Vigo
sites.description: This modern park in the city center offers green lawns, playgrounds, and fountains, making it a popular spot for locals to relax. It connects several neighborhoods and hosts outdoor events during warmer months.
sites.seasonal_notes: Lively during spring and summer festivals

sites.name: Monte Alba Viewpoint
sites.description: Offering sweeping views of Vigo’s bay and cityscape, Monte Alba is a favored spot for sunset watchers and photographers. The viewpoint is accessible by a short hike and is surrounded by natural greenery.
sites.seasonal_notes: Clear skies in summer and autumn provide the best visibility
sites.booking_tips: No booking necessary; bring water and comfortable shoes

sites.name: Museo Quiñones de León
sites.description: Housed in the Pazo Quiñones de León, this museum showcases Galician art and historical artifacts within a beautifully preserved Baroque palace. The grounds and gardens complement the cultural experience.
sites.seasonal_notes: Gardens are most attractive in spring and summer

sites.name: Playa de Samil
sites.description: Vigo’s largest and most popular beach, Playa de Samil offers fine sand, playgrounds, and a promenade lined with cafes. It’s a hotspot for swimming, sunbathing, and outdoor sports during the summer.
sites.seasonal_notes: Peak season from June to September; facilities may close off-season
sites.booking_tips: No booking required; arrive early on warm weekends

sites.name: Centro Cultural Caixanova
sites.description: A hub for contemporary art and cultural exhibitions in Vigo, this center hosts rotating art shows, concerts, and workshops. The modern architecture contrasts with the historic old town nearby.
sites.seasonal_notes: Exhibitions change frequently; check website for current shows
sites.booking_tips: Free entry; book special event tickets in advance

sites.name: Mirador del Monte de O Castro
sites.description: This viewpoint on the Monte de O Castro hill provides sweeping views over Vigo’s port, city center, and the Atlantic Ocean. It’s accessible via a scenic walk through the park and is popular for photography.
sites.seasonal_notes: Clear days provide best visibility; avoid foggy winter mornings

neighborhoods.name: Casco Vello
neighborhoods.character: Casco Vello is the historic heart of Vigo, where narrow cobblestone streets wind through centuries-old buildings, creating a charming and atmospheric setting. The vibe is a mix of old-world charm and lively local life, with plazas buzzing with cafes and traditional shops.

location.description: Located in the very center of Vigo, near the estuary and port area.

history.overview: Casco Vello is the original medieval nucleus of Vigo, dating back to Roman and medieval times.
history.significance: It represents the city’s roots and hosts many preserved historic sites reflecting Vigo’s maritime and cultural heritage.

practical_info.safety: Generally safe, with usual urban precautions at night
practical_info.best_time_to_visit: Spring to early autumn for pleasant weather and festivals

attractions.name: Concatedral de Santa María
attractions.description: A beautiful Gothic-style cathedral dating back to the 14th century.
attractions.appeal: Its historic architecture and central location make it a must-see.

attractions.name: Museo do Mar de Galicia
attractions.description: Museum showcasing Galicia’s rich maritime history and culture.
attractions.appeal: Insightful exhibits on the sea’s influence on local life.

insider_tips: Visit early morning to enjoy peaceful streets and local markets.
Try the local 'empanada' at small bakeries off the main squares.
Check out the small artisan shops for unique souvenirs.

neighborhoods.name: Areal
neighborhoods.character: Areal is Vigo’s bustling commercial and nightlife hub, featuring wide avenues lined with shops, bars, and restaurants. It has a lively, youthful energy with a trendy vibe attracting locals and visitors alike.

location.description: Just west of Casco Vello, stretching along Avenida de García Barbón.

history.overview: Developed in the 19th and 20th centuries as a commercial artery connecting the city center to newer districts.
history.significance: It evolved as a social and economic hub, shaping Vigo’s modern urban identity.

practical_info.safety: Safe with vibrant street life, though watch out in late hours
practical_info.best_time_to_visit: Evenings for nightlife, weekends for shopping
practical_info.walkability: Very walkable with wide sidewalks and pedestrian crossings

attractions.name: Teatro Afundación
attractions.description: Popular venue hosting plays, concerts, and cultural events.
attractions.appeal: Experience local performing arts and vibrant cultural scenes.

dining.name: O Portón
dining.known_for: Creative seafood dishes and elegant presentations

insider_tips: Explore side streets for hidden cafes with local charm.
Weekday evenings offer less crowded bars for a relaxed vibe.
Look out for street performances near the Gran Vía on weekends.

neighborhoods.name: El Calvario
neighborhoods.character: El Calvario is a hip and artsy neighborhood perched on the hillside overlooking the city and Ría de Vigo. Its colorful murals, bohemian cafes, and creative energy make it a magnet for young locals and artists.

location.description: Northwest of the city center, on a hill providing panoramic views.

history.overview: Once a working-class district, it has transformed into a cultural hotspot in recent decades.
history.significance: Known for its grassroots artistic movements and community projects revitalizing the area.

practical_info.safety: Generally safe, with an open and welcoming community
practical_info.best_time_to_visit: Spring and summer for outdoor art and festivals
practical_info.walkability: Walkable but hilly terrain requires moderate fitness

attractions.name: Mirador del Monte do Castro
attractions.description: Offers sweeping views over Vigo and the estuary.
attractions.appeal: Perfect for sunset photography and city panoramas.

insider_tips: Visit during local street art festivals to see live mural paintings.
Walk up to the viewpoint at sunset for breathtaking vistas.
Try local pastries at small bakeries tucked away in side streets.

neighborhoods.name: Bouzas
neighborhoods.character: Bouzas is a charming fishing village turned residential neighborhood with a laid-back seaside vibe. Its narrow streets, traditional houses, and bustling fish market reflect a strong maritime heritage.

location.description: Southwest of the city center along the coast, near the port entrance.

history.overview: Historically a fishing village, Bouzas was incorporated into Vigo as the city expanded.
history.significance: It preserves traditional Galician fishing culture within a modern urban setting.
notable_events: Integration into Vigo municipality in 20th century

practical_info.best_time_to_visit: Summer for beach activities and festivals

attractions.name: Bouzas Fish Market
attractions.description: Bustling market selling fresh seafood directly from fishing boats.
attractions.appeal: Experience the local fishing tradition and buy fresh catch.

attractions.name: Playa de Bouzas
attractions.description: Sandy urban beach popular with locals for swimming and sunbathing.
attractions.appeal: Great spot to relax after exploring the neighborhood.

dining.name: Restaurante Maruja Limón
dining.known_for: Creative seafood dishes with local ingredients

insider_tips: Visit early morning for the freshest seafood at the market.
Walk along the promenade to see traditional fishing boats and local life.
Attend the Bouzas Maritime Festival in August for lively celebrations.

neighborhoods.name: Coia
neighborhoods.character: Coia is a primarily residential neighborhood with a mix of modern apartment blocks and green parks, offering a quieter local atmosphere away from the city bustle. It’s family-friendly and known for its community sports facilities and open spaces.

location.description: Northwest edge of Vigo, bordering the estuary and hills.

history.overview: Originally rural land, Coia was developed in the mid-20th century to meet housing demands.
history.significance: It represents Vigo’s urban expansion and serves as a green residential zone.

practical_info.safety: Safe neighborhood with low crime rates
practical_info.best_time_to_visit: Year-round for park visits and sports events

attractions.name: Parque de Castrelos
attractions.description: Large green space with gardens, walking paths, and an open-air amphitheater.
attractions.appeal: Ideal for picnics, jogging, and cultural events.

attractions.name: Estadio Balaídos
attractions.description: Home stadium of Celta de Vigo football club.
attractions.appeal: Catch a local soccer match or tour the stadium.

dining.name: Mesón O Lagar
dining.known_for: Hearty regional dishes and friendly service

insider_tips: Visit on match days to experience local football passion.
Explore the park on weekends for local outdoor events and markets.
Use local bus lines to connect to the city center efficiently.

neighborhoods.name: O Berbés
neighborhoods.character: O Berbés is Vigo’s traditional fishermen’s quarter with a gritty charm and maritime soul. The neighborhood is characterized by colorful houses, narrow alleys, and a working harbor atmosphere steeped in authentic Galician culture.

location.description: East of Casco Vello, along the waterfront near the Port of Vigo.

history.overview: Historically the fishing district and port workers’ neighborhood, maintaining a close connection to Vigo’s maritime economy.
history.significance: It preserves the city’s fishing traditions and is a living testament to its seafaring heritage.

practical_info.safety: Safe with an active working harbor environment
practical_info.best_time_to_visit: Spring through autumn for market and harbor activity
practical_info.walkability: Highly walkable but some uneven terrain near docks

attractions.name: Mercado de la Piedra
attractions.description: Vibrant market where fishermen sell the day’s fresh catch.
attractions.appeal: Experience the freshest seafood and local market atmosphere.

insider_tips: Arrive early to see fishermen unloading fresh catches.
Try the oysters and clams at the market stalls.
Walk along the docks for great photo opportunities of boats and harbor life.

fine_dining.name: Maruja Limón
fine_dining.description: A Michelin-starred restaurant offering innovative takes on traditional Galician seafood and seasonal ingredients with elegant presentation.
fine_dining.atmosphere: Chic and intimate with modern decor
fine_dining.local_tips: Try the tasting menu for the best seasonal specialties paired with local wines.

fine_dining.name: O Portón
fine_dining.description: Elegant restaurant specializing in fresh, locally sourced seafood with a focus on traditional flavors elevated by refined techniques.
fine_dining.atmosphere: Upscale and cozy with nautical touches
fine_dining.local_tips: Reserve a table by the window to enjoy views of the historic old town while dining.

fine_dining.name: A Pedra
fine_dining.description: A refined dining experience focusing on traditional Galician dishes with a modern twist, featuring superb seafood and seasonal produce.
fine_dining.atmosphere: Warm and elegant with contemporary styling
fine_dining.local_tips: Their octopus with paprika and olive oil is a must-try local classic.

casual_dining.name: Taberna A Pedra
casual_dining.description: A lively tavern serving classic Galician tapas and raciones with a focus on fresh seafood and local ingredients.
casual_dining.atmosphere: Bustling and informal with a friendly vibe
casual_dining.local_tips: Try the patatas con chocos (potatoes with cuttlefish), a regional favorite.

casual_dining.name: Casa Marco
casual_dining.description: Classic Galician cuisine with an emphasis on grilled meats and fresh seafood served in a cozy, family-friendly setting.
casual_dining.local_tips: Their grilled sardines are a local staple, best enjoyed with a glass of Albariño.

casual_dining.name: El Refugio
casual_dining.description: Casual eatery known for its fresh seafood dishes and extensive tapas menu, popular with locals for everyday dining.
casual_dining.local_tips: Sit at the bar for the best interaction with staff and quick service.

casual_dining.name: La Taberna de Lino
casual_dining.description: Popular spot for traditional Galician dishes and Spanish classics with a casual, friendly atmosphere.
casual_dining.local_tips: Order the empanada gallega, a savory pie filled with tuna or meat.

casual_dining.name: Mesón A Curva
casual_dining.description: Known for hearty Galician fare including stews, fresh fish, and traditional breads in a relaxed setting.
casual_dining.local_tips: Try the caldo gallego, a warming local broth perfect in cooler months.

street_food.name: Mercado de la Piedra
street_food.description: Vibrant market where you can select fresh seafood and enjoy it prepared on-site at various tapas stalls.
street_food.local_tips: Try the ostras (oysters) and navajas (razor clams) freshly shucked in front of you.

street_food.name: Praza da Constitución Market
street_food.description: Historic square hosting markets with stands offering local cheeses, meats, and street food specialties.
street_food.local_tips: Visit on weekends for the best variety of artisanal products and fresh galician pastries.

street_food.name: Pulpería A Pedra
street_food.description: Street vendor specializing in pulpo a la gallega, a signature Galician dish with boiled octopus, paprika, and olive oil.
street_food.local_tips: Enjoy pulpo with a glass of local white wine for an authentic experience.

bars.name: Kremlino
bars.description: Trendy bar known for creative cocktails and a lively nightlife atmosphere in the heart of Vigo.
bars.local_tips: Try their signature gin-based cocktails infused with local herbs.

bars.name: A Travesía
bars.description: Cozy wine bar offering an excellent selection of Galician wines paired with light tapas.
bars.local_tips: Ask for the Albariño recommendations from the knowledgeable staff.

bars.name: El Contrabajo
bars.description: Eclectic bar with a strong focus on craft beers and inventive cocktails, popular among locals and visitors.
bars.local_tips: Check their rotating taps for unique Galician craft beer options.

cafes.name: Café Bar Eléctrico
cafes.description: Popular café with specialty coffee, homemade pastries, and a charming vintage ambiance.
cafes.local_tips: Try their artisanal croissants along with a cortado.

cafes.name: La Flor de la Canela
cafes.description: Charming café known for its fresh baked goods, Galician-style cakes, and excellent coffee selection.
cafes.local_tips: Sample the tarta de Santiago, a traditional almond cake.

cafes.name: Café Moderno
cafes.description: Historic café with a vintage feel, serving excellent coffee, sandwiches, and light meals in a relaxed setting.
cafes.local_tips: A great spot to enjoy a coffee while watching the city life in the Plaza de la Constitución.

food_experiences.name: Vigo Tapas & Wine Walking Tour
food_experiences.description: Guided tour exploring Vigo’s best tapas bars and local wines, including tastings of signature dishes and insights into Galician food culture.

food_experiences.name: Galician Seafood Cooking Class
food_experiences.description: Hands-on cooking experience focusing on preparing traditional seafood dishes like pulpo a la gallega and caldeirada with expert chefs.

food_experiences.name: Mercado de la Piedra Fresh Seafood Market Visit
food_experiences.description: Visit this bustling seafood market with a local guide to learn about the region’s famous shellfish and fish varieties, followed by a tasting session.

food_experiences.name: Albariño Wine Tasting Experience
food_experiences.description: An immersive tasting session featuring the renowned Albariño wines from nearby Rías Baixas paired with local cheeses and seafood.

spring: Lamprea (lamprey eel) dishes, a seasonal delicacy served in traditional Galician style
Fresh shellfish like clams and cockles from the Rías Baixas
Wild asparagus, often grilled or incorporated into omelets
summer: Grilled sardines, a popular summer street food in Vigo
Fresh oysters and razor clams from local markets
Chilled gazpacho with local tomatoes and peppers
autumn: Setas (wild mushrooms) harvested from nearby forests
Chestnuts roasted or used in desserts
Pulpo a la gallega (octopus) prepared during local harvest festivals
winter: Caldo gallego, a hearty broth with greens, potatoes, and beans
Stews featuring locally sourced meats and root vegetables
Turrón and polvorones, traditional sweets during holiday season

transport_types.notes: Alvia trains connect Vigo to Madrid Chamartín station; comfortable and scenic.
Direct buses available from Vigo bus station to Madrid Estación Sur.
Direct flights from Vigo Airport to Madrid-Barajas.
Frequent direct buses from Vigo to Porto Campanhã station.
Requires transfer at Valença; scenic route along the border.
Direct flights from Vigo to Lisbon Humberto Delgado Airport.
Overnight bus options available.
Direct trains from Vigo to Santiago; frequent service.
Frequent buses connecting the two cities.
Direct flights from Vigo Airport to Barcelona-El Prat.
Overnight buses available.
Direct flights from Vigo to Paris Charles de Gaulle or Orly.
Long-distance bus with transfers; less frequent.
Direct flights from Vigo to London Stansted and Gatwick.
Direct flights from Vigo Airport to Bilbao Airport.
Day and overnight buses available.
Frequent direct buses.
Multiple daily trains.
Ferry connects Vigo to Saint-Nazaire; weekly service, popular for car transport.
Bus connections available with transfers.

Spring.name: Festa da Reconquista
Spring.description: A vibrant historical festival celebrating Vigo's reconquest in 1809 with parades, reenactments, and street performances.

Spring.name: Stroll in Castrelos Park
Spring.description: Enjoy blooming gardens and outdoor sculptures in one of Vigo's largest green spaces, perfect for picnics and relaxing walks.

Spring.name: Seafood tasting at O Berbés Market
Spring.description: Sample fresh spring shellfish and fish at the traditional market situated near the port.

Spring.name: Hiking the Cíes Islands
Spring.description: Take a ferry to the Cíes Islands for scenic hiking trails amid unique flora and stunning Atlantic views.

Summer.name: Festa do Marisco (Seafood Festival)
Summer.description: One of the largest seafood festivals in Spain featuring local Galician dishes, live music, and cultural events.

Summer.name: Beach day at Samil Beach
Summer.description: Relax or swim at Vigo's most popular urban beach, equipped with facilities and beachfront cafes.

Summer.name: Boat tour around Vigo estuary
Summer.description: Explore the coastline and nearby islands by boat, enjoying panoramic views and marine wildlife spotting.

Summer.name: Open-air concerts at Praza da Constitución
Summer.description: Attend free or low-cost live music performances in the city center during summer evenings.

Summer.name: Kayaking in Ría de Vigo
Summer.description: Rent a kayak to explore the calm waters of Vigo's ria, perfect for all skill levels.

Autumn.name: Harvest seafood dishes featuring octopus and shellfish
Autumn.description: Try seasonal delicacies like pulpo a la gallega and fresh shellfish at local restaurants during the autumn harvest.

Autumn.name: Vigo International Film Festival (FICVI)
Autumn.description: Attend screenings and events showcasing Galician and international cinema.

Autumn.name: Hiking in Monte Alba
Autumn.description: Enjoy cooler weather and colorful foliage on trails offering panoramic views of Vigo and the estuary.

Autumn.name: Visit the Museum of the Sea
Autumn.description: Explore exhibits about Vigo’s maritime heritage and fishing industry, ideal for cooler autumn days.
Autumn.location: Museo do Mar de Galicia, Vigo

Winter.name: Christmas Market at Praza da Constitución
Winter.description: Experience festive stalls selling crafts, seasonal foods, and local products in the heart of Vigo.

Winter.name: Taste winter Galician stews and seafood
Winter.description: Warm up with hearty dishes like caldo gallego and freshly prepared shellfish served in cozy local taverns.

Winter.name: Visit the García Barbón Theatre
Winter.description: Attend winter performances including classical music, theater, and dance at this historic venue.

Winter.name: Walk along the Vigo Waterfront
Winter.description: Enjoy crisp winter air and views of the Atlantic Ocean from the promenade, often quieter in winter months.

Winter.name: Indoor climbing at Esportiu Vigo
Winter.description: Stay active during colder months with indoor climbing facilities suitable for all levels.

ranges.notes: Cool and mild weather with occasional rain; Epiphany celebrations bring local cultural events but crowds remain low to moderate.
Coldest part of the year with frequent rain and grey skies; low tourist activity and limited outdoor options.
Continued chilly weather and rain; indoor cultural sites more appealing; off-season prices attract budget travelers.
Cold and wet with short daylight; few events, but prices remain low; good for budget travelers who don’t mind weather.

ranges.event: Carnaval de Vigo (dates vary, often mid-February)
ranges.notes: Carnival brings lively parades and street events; weather still cool but festive atmosphere improves appeal.
Post-carnival quieter days; weather improving slightly but still cool and rainy; good for cultural visits and lower costs.
Spring begins with mild temperatures; some rain persists but daylight lengthens; ideal for walking and sightseeing with fewer tourists.

ranges.event: St. Joseph's Day (Día de San José) - March 19
ranges.notes: Spring festivals start; weather improves with more sunny days; good time for outdoor activities and local fiestas.
Warming weather with occasional rain; early spring flowers and fewer crowds; ideal for hiking and exploring the Rías Baixas coastline.
Generally mild and pleasant weather with spring blooms; occasional showers; good for outdoor sightseeing and moderate crowds.

ranges.event: Semana Santa (Easter Holy Week - dates vary, often April)
ranges.notes: Major religious celebrations with processions; vibrant local traditions; mild weather with increased visitors but not overwhelming.
Post-Easter calm with pleasant temperatures; great for nature walks and visiting nearby islands; lower crowd levels than Easter week.
Warm, sunny weather with numerous local festivals and cultural events; perfect for beach visits and outdoor dining.
Consistently warm and dry with long daylight hours; ideal time for hiking, beaches, and exploring vineyards and seafood markets.
Start of summer with warm temperatures and low rainfall; beaches become popular but not overcrowded yet.
Warmest weather with festival atmosphere; rising tourist numbers but still manageable; great for beach and cultural experiences.
High summer with hot and sunny days, occasional humidity; beaches crowded; good nightlife but higher prices and busy venues.
Popular local festival celebrating the city’s history; hot weather persists; vibrant but crowded, especially around event venues.
Peak summer with warm to hot weather; beaches busy; city vibrant with tourists; some locals on vacation reducing business traffic.
Late August sees slight drop in temperatures; many locals still on holiday; quieter business scene; some tourist fatigue begins.
Warm weather continues with fewer crowds; Sea Fest celebrates seafood and marine culture attracting food lovers.
Pleasant temperatures with reduced rainfall; ideal for hiking and cultural visits; crowds diminish as summer tourists depart.
Cooling temperatures and increased rain; autumn colors appear; fewer tourists and affordable accommodation.

ranges.event: Día de la Hispanidad / National Day (October 12)
ranges.notes: National holiday with some local events; weather can be wet and cool; good for cultural exploration with limited crowds.
Cool and rainy with shorter days; low tourist activity; museums and indoor attractions are best; budget-friendly time to visit.
Continued wet and chilly weather; few events; excellent for budget travelers but limited outdoor activities.
Chilly weather with festive holiday decorations starting; moderate rain; quieter than major cities but charming Christmas markets.
Holiday season in full swing with markets, lights, and cultural events; cool weather but great atmosphere for couples and families.
Festive celebrations with local traditions; cold and possibly rainy; some businesses closed during holidays; moderate tourist presence.
